heroOverview: Hypercarotinemia is an excess of carotenoid pigments in the bloodstream, almost always caused by consuming large quantities of carotene-rich foods such as carrots, sweet potatoes, and dark leafy greens. The hallmark sign is a yellowish-orange discoloration of the skin, most visible on the palms, soles, and nasolabial folds, while the sclerae remain white — a key difference from jaundice. The condition affects an estimated 3–10% of people who follow heavily vegetable-based or supplement-heavy diets. By itself, dietary hypercarotinemia is harmless and fully reversible by reducing intake over weeks to months. However, when it occurs in the absence of obvious dietary excess, it may signal an underlying metabolic or endocrine disorder, most commonly hypothyroidism, diabetes mellitus, or anorexia nervosa, where impaired conversion of beta-carotene to vitamin A allows carotene to accumulate in the blood.

keyStats.prevalence: Exact prevalence is poorly quantified; subclinical elevation of serum carotene is common in adults eating high-vegetable diets; clinically visible carotenodermia is estimated to affect 3–10% of people with prolonged high carotenoid intake
keyStats.demographics: Affects all ages; infants fed pureed carrot-based foods, children on plant-rich diets, and adults following vegetable-heavy or supplement-heavy dietary patterns are most commonly affected
keyStats.avgAge: No strong age predilection for dietary form; non-dietary cases (hypothyroidism, diabetes) peak in middle-aged adults
keyStats.globalCases: Poorly tracked globally as a standalone diagnosis; reported worldwide in populations with high carotenoid food intake

definition: Hypercarotinemia (ICD-10: E67.1) is a state of elevated plasma carotenoid concentration, most often beta-carotene, that manifests clinically as carotenodermia — yellowish-orange pigmentation of the skin. Carotenoids are fat-soluble plant pigments absorbed from the diet and normally converted in the intestinal mucosa and liver to retinol (vitamin A). When intake exceeds conversion capacity, or when conversion is impaired, unmetabolised carotenoids deposit in the stratum corneum of skin where sebaceous gland density is highest. The condition falls under endocrinology and nutrition medicine, and it is classified under other nutritional excess disorders in ICD-10. Unlike hyperbilirubinaemia, which causes true jaundice with sclerae involvement, hypercarotinemia leaves the sclerae unaffected. Serum beta-carotene above approximately 200 micrograms per decilitre is generally considered elevated, though visible carotenodermia typically requires sustained levels above 250–300 mcg/dL. The condition is entirely distinct from vitamin A toxicity (hypervitaminosis A): carotene itself is not toxic because its conversion to retinol is tightly regulated by feedback, and no cases of vitamin A toxicity have been documented from dietary carotene alone.

typesClassification.description: The most common form, resulting from prolonged consumption of large quantities of carotene-rich foods — particularly carrots, sweet potatoes, squash, pumpkin, and dark green vegetables — or from high-dose carotenoid supplements. Serum levels normalise within weeks of reducing intake.

Accumulation driven by impaired conversion of beta-carotene to vitamin A rather than excess intake; seen in hypothyroidism, poorly controlled diabetes mellitus, anorexia nervosa, and liver disease. The dietary history is unremarkable and the underlying condition requires treatment.

A variant in which lycopene — the red carotenoid in tomatoes — accumulates, giving the skin a more reddish-orange rather than yellow tint. Caused by very high tomato product consumption and is similarly benign.

primarySymptoms: Yellow-orange discoloration of the skin (carotenodermia), appearing first and most prominently on the palms, soles, and nasolabial folds where the stratum corneum is thickest.
Skin discoloration that spares the sclerae (whites of the eyes), distinguishing it from jaundice where eye-whites turn yellow.
Generalised yellowish tinge to the face and body in severe or prolonged cases, sometimes prompting concern about liver disease.
Dry or slightly rough skin texture in some individuals, related to the associated high vegetable intake rather than the carotene itself.
Complete absence of systemic symptoms in dietary cases — no fatigue, nausea, or organ dysfunction.

In secondary hypercarotinemia, symptoms of the underlying condition: fatigue, weight gain, and cold intolerance in hypothyroidism; polydipsia and polyuria in diabetes; food restriction and low body weight in anorexia nervosa.
Incidental finding of elevated serum carotene on a blood panel ordered for unrelated reasons.
Parental concern after noticing orange skin discoloration in an infant or toddler fed pureed carrot or sweet potato.
earlyWarningSigns: Subtle yellowing first noticed on the palms or the skin fold beside the nose before more widespread discoloration appears.
Family members or healthcare providers commenting on a yellowish skin tone in someone eating a high-vegetable diet.
Unexplained carotenodermia in someone who does not eat large amounts of carotene-rich food — this warrants thyroid and glucose testing.
Persistent skin discoloration despite dietary reduction over more than two months, suggesting an underlying metabolic cause.
emergencySigns: Yellow discoloration of the sclerae alongside skin yellowing — this indicates true jaundice from liver or haemolytic disease, not hypercarotinemia, and requires urgent evaluation.
Skin yellowing with abdominal pain, dark urine, or pale stools, which are signs of biliary or hepatic pathology.
Skin discoloration in a newborn — neonatal jaundice is a medical emergency and must be assessed immediately regardless of suspected dietary cause.
Rapid-onset skin yellowing not clearly explained by diet, especially with fatigue, weight loss, or systemic illness.

causes.description: Eating very large quantities of carotene-rich foods — particularly raw or juiced carrots, sweet potatoes, pumpkin, butternut squash, and dark leafy greens — over weeks provides more beta-carotene than the intestinal enzymes can convert to retinol, causing the remainder to circulate and deposit in skin.

Beta-carotene supplements at doses of 15 mg per day or above, often taken as 'safe' alternatives to vitamin A or as antioxidants, can deliver carotenoid loads that far exceed what could be achieved through diet alone, leading to rapid elevation of plasma levels.

Thyroid hormone up-regulates the intestinal enzyme that cleaves beta-carotene into two molecules of retinal. In hypothyroid states this enzymatic step is slowed, so even a normal dietary carotenoid intake accumulates in the blood and manifests as skin discoloration.

Both type 1 and type 2 diabetes impair beta-carotene dioxygenase activity in the intestinal wall, reducing conversion efficiency and allowing circulating carotene to rise to visible levels even without excessive intake.

Despite very low caloric intake overall, some individuals with anorexia preferentially consume large amounts of yellow-orange vegetables, and impaired hepatic carotene metabolism in the malnourished state compounds the accumulation.

Significant hepatic dysfunction impairs the second step of carotenoid metabolism that occurs in the liver, allowing elevated plasma carotene even when dietary intake is modest and thyroid function is normal.
Hormonal changes can modestly alter carotenoid metabolism; elevated carotene levels have been observed in pregnancy, though true carotenodermia is uncommon.

riskFactors.description: Vegan, vegetarian, and raw-food diets typically contain far higher carotenoid loads than omnivorous diets; individuals juicing multiple carrots daily are at particularly high risk of visible skin discoloration.
Supplementing with 15–50 mg beta-carotene daily — doses used in some cancer-prevention trials — reliably raises serum carotene to levels that produce carotenodermia within weeks.
Both overt and subclinical hypothyroidism reduce the rate of carotene-to-retinol conversion; this is the most common metabolic cause of hypercarotinemia diagnosed in clinical practice.
Poor glycaemic control is associated with impaired intestinal carotene cleavage; tighter glucose management often allows carotene levels to fall even without dietary change.
Nutritional rehabilitation typically normalises carotene metabolism, but during active illness the combination of selective intake and impaired metabolism compounds the risk.

Infants fed pureed carrot, sweet potato, and squash as first foods can develop visible carotenodermia quickly because their smaller body mass and higher relative carotenoid intake per kilogram accelerates accumulation.

Women have modestly higher plasma carotenoid levels than men at equivalent intake, possibly related to differences in fat distribution, where carotene is stored, and in hormonal influences on metabolism.
Dietary fat enhances carotenoid absorption; consuming carotene-rich foods with oils or fatty foods increases the fraction absorbed, raising peak serum levels.

affectedDemographics: Infants and toddlers receiving high proportions of carrot- and squash-based pureed foods
Adults following plant-based, raw-food, or juice-cleanse diets
Individuals taking beta-carotene supplements at doses above 15 mg per day
People with untreated or poorly controlled hypothyroidism
Adults with poorly controlled diabetes mellitus
Individuals with anorexia nervosa or other restrictive eating disorders
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of hypercarotinemia is primarily clinical: yellow-orange skin discoloration that spares the sclerae in a patient with an identifiable dietary excess is sufficient to make the diagnosis without laboratory confirmation. A detailed dietary history — asking specifically about daily intake of carrots, sweet potatoes, squash, dark green vegetables, and carotenoid supplements — is the most important diagnostic step. When the dietary history is insufficient to explain the degree of discoloration, or when the dietary cause seems unlikely, a serum beta-carotene level confirms elevation and prompts investigation for a secondary cause. A thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) level and fasting glucose or HbA1c should be measured in any patient with non-dietary hypercarotinemia, as hypothyroidism and diabetes are the most common underlying conditions. Liver function tests are checked when hepatic disease is suspected. The critical distinction from jaundice is clinical: bilirubin elevation causes scleral icterus, which carotenodermia does not. When doubt exists, a serum bilirubin is measured — it is normal in pure hypercarotinemia. Skin biopsy is not needed but, if performed for another reason, shows carotenoid deposits in the superficial dermis and stratum corneum without inflammation. In infants, the diagnosis is made after excluding neonatal jaundice, which is a different and urgent condition.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms elevated circulating carotenoid concentration; levels above approximately 200 mcg/dL are considered elevated, and carotenodermia typically appears above 250–300 mcg/dL.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A routine blood draw from the arm; the sample should ideally be taken fasting. Results are usually available within one to two business days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes jaundice from liver disease or haemolysis; bilirubin is normal in pure hypercarotinemia, providing the definitive distinction.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Part of the same blood panel; no special preparation required unless a full liver function test is ordered alongside it.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for hypothyroidism, the most common endocrine cause of secondary hypercarotinemia; an elevated TSH confirms underactive thyroid function.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; results are available the same or next day. A raised result prompts measurement of free thyroxine (FT4) to gauge severity.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for undiagnosed or poorly controlled diabetes mellitus, which impairs carotene-to-retinol conversion.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Fasting glucose requires an overnight fast before the blood draw; HbA1c does not. Both are part of a routine metabolic panel.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses hepatic health when liver disease is suspected as a contributing factor; elevated transaminases or bilirubin indicate hepatic rather than dietary aetiology.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood panel; results are usually available the same day. No preparation is required beyond avoiding alcohol for 24 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Dietary history and food diary review
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies specific high-carotene foods, juices, and supplements driving the excess; this is frequently the only investigation needed in straightforward dietary cases.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician or dietitian asks detailed questions about typical daily food and supplement intake over the past one to three months. No blood tests or imaging are required.

treatmentOverview: The treatment of dietary hypercarotinemia is straightforward: reduce intake of carotene-rich foods and stop carotenoid supplements, and the skin discoloration fades within four to six weeks as plasma levels normalise. There is no need for medication. Clinicians should first verify the diagnosis — specifically exclude jaundice — and take a careful dietary history so the patient understands exactly which foods to moderate rather than eliminate entirely. A sustained moderate diet that remains rich in a variety of vegetables is healthier than swinging to very low intake, and vegetables need not be avoided altogether; simply reducing the specific high-carotene items and pausing supplements is sufficient. When secondary hypercarotinemia is identified, treatment is directed at the underlying condition: thyroid hormone replacement for hypothyroidism, improved glycaemic management for diabetes, and nutritional rehabilitation under multidisciplinary care for anorexia nervosa. As the underlying condition is controlled, carotene levels typically fall without requiring further dietary restriction. Patient reassurance is an important part of management: many patients presenting with yellow skin fear liver failure, and explaining the benign and reversible nature of carotenodermia, along with its straightforward dietary cause, significantly reduces anxiety and improves adherence to the recommended changes. Serial serum beta-carotene measurements at six-week intervals confirm that levels are declining and provide objective reassurance to both patient and clinician. Dietary consultation with a registered dietitian is valuable when the patient relies heavily on plant-based eating and needs help recalibrating carotenoid load without compromising overall nutritional quality or inadvertently creating new deficiencies.

medicalTreatments.name: Dietary reduction of carotenoid-rich foods
medicalTreatments.description: The primary treatment for dietary hypercarotinemia. Patients are guided to reduce carrots, sweet potatoes, pumpkin, butternut squash, and dark leafy greens to moderate quantities, and to stop high-dose carotenoid supplements. A dietitian review can help calibrate a balanced plan.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Skin discoloration resolves in 4–6 weeks in approximately 95% of patients once serum carotene falls below approximately 200 mcg/dL.

medicalTreatments.name: Thyroid hormone replacement (levothyroxine)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line treatment for secondary hypercarotinemia caused by hypothyroidism. Restoring normal thyroid hormone levels re-activates the enzyme converting beta-carotene to retinol, allowing carotene to clear even without strict dietary restriction.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Once euthyroidism is achieved — typically within 4–8 weeks of reaching the correct levothyroxine dose — carotene levels fall by approximately 40–60% in most patients.

medicalTreatments.name: Glycaemic optimisation for diabetes
medicalTreatments.description: Improving blood glucose control in patients with diabetes-related secondary hypercarotinemia through medication adjustment, dietary changes, and monitoring. Better control restores intestinal conversion efficiency.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical reports indicate that carotenodermia resolves in most patients with diabetes once HbA1c is brought below 8%, though controlled trial data are limited.

medicalTreatments.name: Nutritional rehabilitation for anorexia nervosa
medicalTreatments.description: Multidisciplinary inpatient or outpatient rehabilitation that restores caloric intake, corrects micronutrient deficiencies, and normalises hepatic metabolism. Carotene levels usually normalise as part of broader metabolic recovery.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Carotenodermia typically resolves within 2–3 months of sustained weight restoration, with near-complete normalisation observed in cohort studies of patients achieving weight recovery.

alternativeTreatments.name: Patient education and reassurance
alternativeTreatments.description: For dietary hypercarotinemia, structured education about which foods contain high carotene levels and why the condition is harmless is often all that is required. Understanding the mechanism improves dietary adherence without creating unnecessary food anxiety.

alternativeTreatments.name: Registered dietitian consultation
alternativeTreatments.description: A dietitian can construct a balanced, colourful, and nutritionally complete eating plan that moderates carotenoid load without eliminating the health benefits of vegetables, particularly valuable for patients who rely on plant-based diets.

whySeeSpecialist: A primary care clinician can manage most cases of dietary hypercarotinemia without specialist referral. An endocrinologist should be involved when the serum TSH is abnormal, when glycaemic control is inadequate, or when carotenodermia persists despite dietary correction, as these scenarios suggest a secondary metabolic cause that needs expert evaluation. A gastroenterologist or hepatologist should be consulted whenever liver disease cannot be excluded on the basis of clinical assessment and standard blood tests. A paediatric endocrinologist may be needed when hypercarotinemia is identified in a child with no clear dietary excess.
doctorSelectionGuide: For straightforward dietary hypercarotinemia, a general practitioner or family physician is appropriate. If a secondary cause is suspected, seek an endocrinologist with experience in thyroid disorders or metabolic disease. Look for a clinician who will take a comprehensive dietary history and order targeted investigations rather than immediately pursuing imaging or invasive tests. For infants and children, a paediatric dietitian familiar with plant-based first-foods and weaning practices adds particular value.
preventionStrategies: Eat a varied diet rather than relying on a narrow range of high-carotene foods; variety dilutes the carotene load while preserving broad nutritional benefit.
Limit carrot juice or vegetable juicing to one 150–200 mL serving per day if you consume it regularly; juicing concentrates carotenoids far beyond what whole vegetables alone provide.
Use carotenoid supplements only at doses recommended in guidelines and for evidence-based indications; avoid prolonged high-dose supplementation.
If you have hypothyroidism or diabetes, maintain regular medical follow-up and treatment compliance to keep conversion of carotene to vitamin A functioning normally.
For infants, introduce carotene-rich purées alongside a variety of other vegetables and foods to prevent disproportionate carotenoid loading during weaning.
lifestyleModifications: Replace daily carrot juice with a mixed vegetable or fruit juice to reduce the carotenoid concentration of each serving.
Cook carotene-rich vegetables rather than eating them raw or juiced; some reduction in carotenoid absorption occurs with cooking, though the effect is modest.
Read supplement labels carefully; many multivitamins, antioxidant formulas, and 'skin health' products contain substantial amounts of beta-carotene.
Keep a brief food diary for two to three weeks to identify which specific foods are driving disproportionate carotene intake.
Monitor skin colour periodically if you follow a plant-rich diet so that gradual changes are noticed early.

recommended: A wide variety of colourful vegetables and fruits — including yellow, orange, red, and green options — in moderate portions
Whole foods rather than concentrated juices or supplements to distribute nutrient load naturally
Adequate dietary fat with meals to support absorption of fat-soluble nutrients other than excessive carotene
avoid: Daily large servings of carrots, sweet potatoes, or butternut squash eaten as the primary vegetable
Regular carrot or vegetable juice in quantities above one glass per day
High-dose standalone beta-carotene supplements (above 6–10 mg per day) without a specific clinical indication
Very narrow mono-vegetable diets that lack diversity

exerciseGuidelines: Exercise does not directly affect carotene metabolism and no specific restrictions or recommendations apply. Maintaining a healthy body weight through regular physical activity supports insulin sensitivity in those with diabetes, which indirectly helps normalise carotene conversion. No modifications to exercise habits are needed for dietary hypercarotinemia.
dailyManagement: Reduce daily intake of the identified high-carotene foods to a portion size of 80–100 g per day rather than eliminating them completely.
Stop any beta-carotene or carotenoid supplement until serum levels have normalised and then discuss with a clinician whether resuming at a lower dose is appropriate.
Take thyroid or diabetes medication as prescribed without interruption if a secondary cause has been identified.
Check skin colour in consistent lighting (natural daylight) weekly to monitor improvement, as the change is gradual.
Return for follow-up in six to eight weeks if skin discoloration has not improved to confirm serum carotene is normalising.
prognosis: The prognosis for dietary hypercarotinemia is excellent. Carotenodermia is entirely benign, causes no organ damage, and resolves completely within four to eight weeks of reducing carotenoid intake. Beta-carotene in dietary quantities has never been shown to cause vitamin A toxicity, and there is no evidence that elevated serum carotene itself harms any organ. For secondary hypercarotinemia, the prognosis depends on the underlying condition: hypothyroidism managed with levothyroxine is a lifelong but well-tolerated condition with normal life expectancy; diabetes requires sustained management but the carotenodermia itself clears once glycaemic control improves. The main risk from the condition as a whole is that yellow skin prompts unnecessary invasive investigation for liver disease before the dietary cause is recognised, creating patient anxiety and cost.
recoveryTimeline: In dietary cases, serum beta-carotene levels begin to fall within one to two weeks of reducing intake, and visible skin discoloration typically fades within four to six weeks, with complete resolution by eight to twelve weeks. In secondary cases, resolution depends on the time required to treat the underlying condition: levothyroxine titration for hypothyroidism takes four to eight weeks to reach a stable dose, after which carotene levels fall over a further four to six weeks. Infants' discoloration clears particularly quickly — often within two to three weeks — once the offending foods are reduced.
complications: Unnecessary invasive testing for liver disease — the most common adverse consequence of hypercarotinemia, caused by failure to recognise the dietary aetiology before ordering imaging or biopsy.
Patient anxiety regarding perceived liver failure or jaundice, which can be significant and is entirely avoidable with prompt accurate diagnosis.
Missed diagnosis of hypothyroidism or diabetes when a clinician attributes the carotenodermia purely to diet without investigating non-dietary causes in susceptible patients.
Nutritional imbalance from over-restricting vegetables in response to the diagnosis, reducing intake of fibre, folate, potassium, and other nutrients that benefit health.

supportResources.name: NIH Office of Dietary Supplements — Vitamin A Fact Sheet for Health Professionals
supportResources.description: Comprehensive review of vitamin A and carotenoid metabolism, upper intake levels, and safety, from the U.S. National Institutes of Health.

supportResources.name: British Thyroid Foundation — Hypothyroidism
supportResources.description: Patient-facing information on hypothyroidism, a leading cause of secondary hypercarotinemia, including symptoms, diagnosis, and treatment.

sources.title: Maharshak N, Shapiro J, Trau H. Carotenodermia — a review of the current literature. International Journal of Dermatology, 2003
NIH Office of Dietary Supplements — Vitamin A Fact Sheet for Health Professionals
Alvarez JA, Micronutrient Deficiencies in Chronic Disease. Endocrinology and Metabolism Clinics of North America, 2020
WHO — Vitamin A supplementation and dietary carotenoid guidance
Biesalski HK. Vitamin A and carotenoids — function, metabolism and toxicology. Annals of Nutrition and Metabolism, 2020

Leung WC, Hessel S, Meplan C et al. Two common single nucleotide polymorphisms in the gene encoding beta-carotene 15,15'-monoxygenase alter the conversion of beta-carotene to retinal. FASEB Journal, 2009

confusedWithConditions.name: Jaundice (unspecified)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Jaundice causes yellow discoloration of both the skin and the sclerae, with elevated serum bilirubin; hypercarotinemia spares the sclerae and bilirubin is normal.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hypervitaminosis A
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Vitamin A toxicity causes headache, bone pain, liver enlargement, and hair loss; hypercarotinemia from dietary carotene causes only skin discoloration and is not toxic.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hypothyroidism
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hypothyroidism can cause secondary hypercarotinemia but also causes fatigue, weight gain, and cold intolerance; the two conditions can coexist and hypothyroidism should be excluded in non-dietary hypercarotinemia.

relatedConditions.name: Hypervitaminosis A
relatedConditions.relevance: Both involve excess fat-soluble substances derived from diet, but only vitamin A toxicity causes organ damage; understanding the distinction prevents unnecessary alarm.

relatedConditions.name: Vitamin A deficiency
relatedConditions.relevance: Hypercarotinemia involves excess precursor to vitamin A; deficiency of vitamin A itself is the opposite extreme and causes night blindness and immune impairment.

relatedConditions.name: Hypothyroidism
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common endocrine cause of secondary hypercarotinemia, acting by impairing beta-carotene-to-retinol conversion.

relatedConditions.name: Disorder of amino acid metabolism (unspecified)
relatedConditions.relevance: Other inborn or acquired metabolic disorders can impair fat-soluble nutrient handling in analogous ways to what thyroid disease does with carotene metabolism.

faqs.question: What causes yellow-orange skin discoloration if it is not jaundice?
faqs.answer: Yellow-orange skin that spares the whites of the eyes is almost always carotenodermia from high dietary intake of carotene-rich foods such as carrots, sweet potatoes, and squash, or from beta-carotene supplements. Unlike jaundice, bilirubin is normal and the sclerae remain white. Reducing the offending foods resolves it within 4–6 weeks.

faqs.question: Is hypercarotinemia dangerous?
faqs.answer: Dietary hypercarotinemia is harmless. Beta-carotene from food has never caused vitamin A toxicity because the body tightly regulates how much it converts to retinol. The only risks are unnecessary tests if doctors mistake it for jaundice, and occasionally missing an underlying cause such as hypothyroidism or diabetes.

faqs.question: How is hypercarotinemia different from jaundice?
faqs.answer: The key difference is the whites of the eyes. Jaundice turns the sclerae yellow because bilirubin deposits there. Carotenodermia leaves the sclerae completely white. A serum bilirubin test — which is normal in hypercarotinemia — provides definitive confirmation.

faqs.question: What foods cause hypercarotinemia?
faqs.answer: Carrots, sweet potatoes, pumpkin, butternut squash, dark leafy greens (kale, spinach), and papaya are the main sources. Carrot juice and vegetable juices are especially concentrated. Carotenoid supplements containing 15 mg or more of beta-carotene daily can also cause visible skin discoloration within weeks.

faqs.question: How long does it take for carotenodermia to go away?
faqs.answer: Once high-carotene foods and supplements are reduced, serum levels begin falling within 1–2 weeks and skin discoloration visibly fades over 4–6 weeks, with complete resolution usually within 8–12 weeks. Infants clear it faster — often in 2–3 weeks.

faqs.question: Can hypothyroidism cause yellow skin?
faqs.answer: Yes. Hypothyroidism impairs the enzyme that converts beta-carotene to vitamin A, so carotene accumulates in the blood even with a normal diet. If carotenodermia occurs without obvious dietary excess, a TSH blood test should be done to rule out underactive thyroid.

faqs.question: Can diabetes cause carotenodermia?
faqs.answer: Yes. Both type 1 and type 2 diabetes reduce intestinal beta-carotene conversion efficiency. Yellow skin in a person with poorly controlled diabetes may be driven by impaired carotene metabolism rather than diet alone. Improving glycaemic control usually resolves it.

faqs.question: Can babies get hypercarotinemia?
faqs.answer: Yes, and it is one of the most common causes of yellow-orange skin in infants. It typically follows heavy introduction of carrot, sweet potato, and squash purées during weaning. The condition is harmless, but neonatal jaundice must always be excluded first, as that is a separate and urgent condition.

faqs.question: Do I need to stop eating vegetables if I have carotenodermia?
faqs.answer: No. Only the specific high-carotene foods need to be moderated, not all vegetables. A varied diet including many different vegetables in normal portions will not maintain elevated carotene levels. A dietitian can help build a colourful, nutritious eating plan that reduces carotene load without eliminating plant foods.

faqs.question: What blood tests are done to diagnose hypercarotinemia?
faqs.answer: A serum beta-carotene level confirms the elevation. Serum bilirubin rules out jaundice. TSH screens for hypothyroidism and fasting glucose or HbA1c screens for diabetes. Liver function tests are added if liver disease is suspected. In straightforward dietary cases the diagnosis is often clinical and no blood tests are strictly required.

faqs.question: Can carrot juice cause orange skin?
faqs.answer: Yes. Carrot juice concentrates carotenoids several times beyond what eating whole carrots delivers and is one of the most efficient ways to raise serum carotene. Even one large glass daily sustained over weeks can produce visible carotenodermia in susceptible individuals.

faqs.question: Is carotenodermia the same as hypercarotinemia?
faqs.answer: Hypercarotinemia is the elevated blood carotene level; carotenodermia is the visible skin discoloration it causes. Most people with hypercarotinemia will develop carotenodermia once levels exceed approximately 250–300 mcg/dL, but mild elevation may exist without visible skin change.

faqs.question: Does hypercarotinemia cause vitamin A toxicity?
faqs.answer: No. The enzyme converting beta-carotene to vitamin A is subject to negative feedback and slows automatically when retinol stores are adequate. No cases of vitamin A toxicity from dietary carotene alone have been documented. Vitamin A toxicity requires preformed retinol from animal sources or supplements, not carotene from vegetables.

faqs.question: Should I see a specialist for yellow skin?
faqs.answer: A general practitioner can diagnose and manage most cases of dietary hypercarotinemia. See a specialist if the dietary cause is unclear, if TSH or glucose tests are abnormal, if skin yellowing persists beyond 8 weeks of dietary reduction, or if jaundice cannot be excluded on clinical grounds.

faqs.question: Can beta-carotene supplements cause orange skin?
faqs.answer: Yes. Supplements containing 15–50 mg of beta-carotene daily — doses used in older cancer-prevention research — reliably raise serum carotene to levels that discolor the skin within weeks. Stopping the supplement resolves the discoloration in 4–8 weeks.

faqs.question: Is hypercarotinemia related to lycopenemia?
faqs.answer: Yes. Lycopenemia is a related condition where lycopene — the red carotenoid found in tomatoes and tomato products — accumulates in the skin, giving a reddish-orange tint rather than the yellow-orange of beta-carotene excess. Both are benign and resolve with dietary reduction.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for secondary hypercarotinemia?
faqs.answer: Treat the underlying condition. For hypothyroidism, levothyroxine replacement normalises thyroid function and restores carotene conversion within weeks. For diabetes, improving glycaemic control has the same effect. For anorexia nervosa, nutritional rehabilitation under multidisciplinary care is required. Strict dietary carotene restriction is not needed once the underlying cause is treated.

faqs.question: Does hypercarotinemia affect the eyes?
faqs.answer: No. Carotenoids do not deposit in the sclerae or affect vision. The absence of yellow discoloration of the whites of the eyes is the diagnostic feature that distinguishes carotenodermia from jaundice. Some carotenoids, particularly lutein and zeaxanthin, are actually beneficial to retinal health.

faqs.question: How common is hypercarotinemia?
faqs.answer: Exact figures are not well established, but subclinical elevation of serum carotene is common in adults on plant-heavy diets. Visible carotenodermia is estimated to affect 3–10% of people with prolonged high carotenoid intake. It is one of the most common benign explanations for skin yellowing seen in primary care.

metaDescription: Hypercarotinemia causes harmless yellow-orange skin from high carotene intake or thyroid disease. Learn symptoms, diagnosis, and how it differs from jaundice.
